patch_instructions: Patch 1 — detail_evidence (ISS-1790, single insertion). In data/wins.json, locate WIN id="026". Find the existing sentence: "Reconvergence at the anti-solar point is the geometric signature of a source at effectively infinite distance (≫ 5,733 km), consistent with a sun at 150 million km." Insert the new paragraph in `inserted_paragraph` (below) IMMEDIATELY AFTER that sentence and IMMEDIATELY BEFORE the existing "Notably, the dome model's own parameter documentation..." sentence. The full replacement_detail_evidence string in this file is the result of doing exactly that — the decider may either apply the insertion as a targeted edit OR overwrite the entire field with replacement_detail_evidence; both produce the same result.
No other WIN-026 fields are modified. No other WINs are modified.

current_field_values.detail_evidence_first_chars: Crepuscular rays are parallel beams made visible by atmospheric scattering. The apparent divergence is a perspective eff…
current_field_values.detail_evidence_last_chars: …sun. A claimed WIN that relies on geometry-at-5,733-km cannot simultaneously treat that altitude as an optical illusion.

insertion_anchor_before: consistent with a sun at 150 million km.
insertion_anchor_after: Notably, the dome model's own parameter documentation
inserted_paragraph: A dome defender may try to symmetrise the case: if crepuscular divergence at the sun is "just perspective," then anticrepuscular convergence at the anti-solar point is just perspective too. The move is rhetorically tidy but geometrically inert. Perspective collapses lines to a vanishing point only where the underlying rays are mutually parallel — that is what a vanishing point is. Parallel rays from an effectively-infinite source project to great-circle arcs that share TWO antipodal vanishing points (the source direction and its sky-antipode), so convergence at BOTH horizons is the visual signature of parallelism, not a separable illusion. A finite-altitude source emits a radial fan of non-parallel rays whose sky projections share the source point but not its antipode; on the opposite side they continue fanning out by ≈ arctan(baseline/altitude) — roughly 1° per 100 km of baseline at 5,733 km — yielding a smear, not a vanishing point. Two-horizon convergence is therefore a discriminator, and no aetheric-refraction caveat about the 5,733 km figure can manufacture parallel rays from a finite source.
current_detail_evidence: Crepuscular rays are parallel beams made visible by atmospheric scattering. The apparent divergence is a perspective effect. Crucially, anticrepuscular rays converge at the anti-solar point simultaneously — a result impossible with a local sun. See Atmospheric Optics and Atmospheric Optics (crepuscular rays) for photographic documentation and scattering physics analysis. A local sun at 5,733 km could not produce rays converging at both horizons simultaneously. Simple geometry: two shadow rays through cloud gaps separated by 5 km at 10 km altitude subtend a ~0.05° angular difference at ground level — a difference that only widens as the observer turns away from the sun. That 0.05° is ~9% of the Sun's angular diameter — small enough that a local 5,733 km sun would look near-parallel for short baselines, but converging rays at the anti-solar point require the rays to remain straight over the full sky hemisphere, which is geometrically incompatible with any finite source. Reconvergence at the anti-solar point is the geometric signature of a source at effectively infinite distance (≫ 5,733 km), consistent with a sun at 150 million km. Notably, the dome model's own parameter documentation (raw-text/05-model.txt) labels the 5,733 km figure as "(optical illusion due to aetheric refraction)" — conceding that WIN-026's claimed confirmation of a physically local sun at this altitude is internally inconsistent: the model characterises 5,733 km as a perceptual artefact of aetheric refraction, not an actual physical location for the sun. A claimed WIN that relies on geometry-at-5,733-km cannot simultaneously treat that altitude as an optical illusion.

holes_addressed: ISS-1790 / WIN-026.c3 advocate (defense_survives=3): the dome defender's strongest move was to argue that anti-solar convergence is itself a perspective effect — symmetric to the perspective-explanation we use for the apparent crepuscular divergence at the sun. If both effects are "just perspective," our anti-solar argument loses its force as a discriminator between a parallel-ray (distant) sun and a radial-fan (local) sun. The inserted paragraph closes this loophole by stating the structural fact: vanishing points at BOTH antipodes are the perspective signature of parallel rays specifically. Non-parallel rays from a finite-altitude point source share their source as a common point but not its antipode; their sky projections continue fanning out on the opposite side at ≈ arctan(baseline/altitude), which at 5,733 km is on the order of 1° per 100 km of baseline — a smear, not a vanishing point. The "perspective cuts both ways" defense is therefore wrong on its own terms, regardless of any aetheric-refraction or "5,733 km is illusory" caveat. After integration, defense_survives should drop to 1–2 (residual: a determined dome-defender can still posit some unspecified "aetheric collimation" mechanism that re-parallelises the rays, but that is unfalsifiable hand-waving rather than a geometric argument).
new_evidence_added: Geometric / projective-geometry argument: parallel lines in 3-space project onto great-circle arcs on the celestial sphere, and the two antipodal points where these arcs cross are the line's direction-vector and its negation — the vanishing points at both ends of the sky. This is standard projective geometry (e.g., Coxeter, "Projective Geometry"; Berger, "Geometry I"). Non-parallel lines do not share both antipodal vanishing points, only one (where they actually intersect, if they do).

Quantitative scale of the fan-out at 5,733 km: angular spread ≈ arctan(baseline/altitude). For a 100 km baseline, that is arctan(100/5733) ≈ 1.0° — directly comparable to the 1.0° figure already used in the multi-station parallax stronger-argument in the curmudgeon review. The same geometry that defeats parallax also defeats anti-solar convergence: a 1° spread per 100 km does not collapse to a vanishing point, regardless of what name we put on the optical effect.

anticipated_objections.objection: You are still assuming Euclidean projection. The dome posits aetheric optics with non-trivial refraction. Inside the dome, rays from the sun are bent by the aetheric medium so that they ARE parallel by the time they reach the lower atmosphere — the vanishing-point geometry then works for anti-solar convergence too. Your "no aetheric caveat can save this" sentence is too aggressive.
anticipated_objections.response: The aetheric-collimation move is unfalsifiable in its current form — the dome model does not specify a refractive-index profile that would (a) turn radial rays from a 5,733 km source into parallel rays at ground level AND (b) leave Mie/Rayleigh scattering, the apparent solar diameter (~0.53°), and the overall sky-brightness pattern unchanged. Each of these constraints alone is tight; the conjunction is either over-determined (no consistent n(r) satisfies all of them) or contains free parameters tuned post-hoc to match observations — at which point anti-solar convergence is "explained" by parameter fit, not predicted. WIN-026 is presented as confirmation of crepuscular ray geometry; once the geometric explanation requires undisclosed aetheric collimation, the WIN is no longer self-supporting. Contrast: the standard model (sun at 1 AU, parallel rays, ordinary scattering) requires no extra free parameters and predicts both crepuscular divergence and anticrepuscular convergence as a single geometric consequence.

anticipated_objections.objection: Two-horizon convergence is not the only signature of parallelism — diverging rays from a near-zenith point source can also "appear" to converge near both horizons because of foreshortening. You are over-stating the geometric strength of the discriminator.
anticipated_objections.response: Foreshortening compresses ANGULAR extent near the horizon, but it does not collapse a fan of non-parallel rays to a single point — it compresses the visible spread to the angular size set by the geometry. Non-parallel rays that span Δθ ≈ arctan(baseline/altitude) at the source remain spread by Δθ near any other point along their projection, including the anti-solar horizon. Foreshortening can make the spread visually small (and thus easy to confuse with a vanishing point in casual observation) only if the underlying angular spread is already small — which is what the rays-are-effectively-parallel limit means. So foreshortening reduces the discriminator quantitatively (you need careful measurement, not casual photography, to tell apart a 5,733 km source from infinity at the anti-solar horizon) but does not abolish it. Multi-station parallax (the curmudgeon's stronger_argument) handles the casual-observation case directly.

kernel_of_truth: The dome defender has noticed a real fact about projective geometry: vanishing points DO occur at both ends of a parallel-line bundle, and "perspective" really does explain BOTH the apparent convergence at the sun direction AND the apparent convergence at the anti-solar direction simultaneously. It is correct to say "if crepuscular ray divergence is perspective, anticrepuscular convergence is perspective too" — these are not two separate optical effects, they are the same effect (parallel-line projective geometry) viewed from two different parts of the celestial sphere. The dome author has identified a genuine symmetry. Why it still does not save WIN-026: this symmetry is exactly the property that makes two-horizon convergence a DISCRIMINATOR. Both vanishing points exist together when (and only when) the rays are mutually parallel. A finite-altitude source produces non-parallel rays whose sky projections share the source point but never its antipode; the symmetric "perspective" the defender invokes does not apply because the underlying lines are not parallel. So the kernel — "perspective explains both ends" — is precisely the geometric fact that rules out a finite-altitude source.
